claims.claim: Orang Asli Blockade Stops Logging in Royal Belum for 87 Days
claims.source: No primary source found for a Royal Belum 87-day blockade after searches for exact phrases and relevant variants. Related verified issue is a 2019 Perak/Gerik blockade, not a late-2025 Royal Belum event: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019, pp. 170-171, https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Do not state the Royal Belum 87-day claim unless a primary source is found. The documented public record supports 2019 blockades in Kg. Sg Papan and Kg. Cunex, Gerik, Perak, not this headline as written.
claims.stale_check: Searches through 2026-05-17 did not find evidence that a later 2025 Royal Belum event made this claim newly accurate.

claims.claim: In late 2025, 200 Temiar villagers established a blockade on logging roads entering Royal Belum State Park's buffer zone, halting operations for 87 days before police dismantled it.
claims.source: No primary source found for late-2025, 200 villagers, Royal Belum buffer zone, 87 days, or police dismantling. Related official human-rights record: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 records Perak blockades in Kg. Sg Papan and Kg. Cunex, Gerik, and three Orang Asli remanded after a 23 July 2019 operation, https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced 2019 Perak/Gerik account or remove. If referring to 2019 Air Cepam/Cunex/Sg Papan events near Royal Belum, state that explicitly and do not use 2025, 200 villagers, or 87 days without evidence.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found that this became true after earlier 2019 reporting.

claims.claim: 200 Temiar villagers blockaded logging roads for 87 days.
claims.source: No primary source found for the exact 200-villager/87-day combination. The Star reported 200 Temiar protesters in a Kelantan/Gua Musang barricade in 2016, not Royal Belum or 87 days: https://www.thestar.com.my/news/nation/2016/10/03/orang-asli-put-up-another-blockade-at-logging-site. This is secondary and not primary.
claims.correction: The number 200 appears to match older Kelantan reporting, while the 87-day duration is not verified. Remove both figures unless a primary source is produced.

claims.source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 says an operation led by the Perak Forestry Department removed a blockade in Kg. Sungai Papan on 23 July 2019 and three Orang Asli were remanded; it does not support a late-2025 Royal Belum police-dismantling claim. https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Attribute the agency and date accurately: SUHAKAM describes a Perak Forestry Department-led operation on 23 July 2019, with police involvement in detention/remand, not the 2025 Royal Belum claim.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found for a newer 2025 police dismantling.

claims.source: No primary source found for logging resuming within 48 hours. Malaysiakini reported the Perak MB saying a logging company was permitted to fell trees and notices were issued in April 2019, but this does not verify a 48-hour resumption: https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409
claims.correction: Remove the 48-hour claim unless a dated official record or court/police/forestry document verifies it.

claims.claim: No consultation with the community followed.
claims.source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 records Orang Asli complaints that logging continued despite concerns and objections, and recommends free, prior and informed consent, but does not verify that no consultation followed the specific alleged 2025 blockade. https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: State only that SUHAKAM recorded complaints of non-action and recommended FPIC, unless a source verifies absence of post-blockade consultation.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found for a 2025 post-blockade consultation timeline.

claims.claim: The Federal Court's 2019 Kelantan ruling recognised Orang Asli customary land rights.
claims.source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 identifies landmark Orang Asli customary land cases as Adong bin Kuwau v Kerajaan Negeri Johor in 1998 and Sagong bin Tasi v Kerajaan Negeri Selangor in 2002; Sagong was a Court of Appeal decision in 2005, not a 2019 Federal Court Kelantan ruling. https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Correct to: Malaysian courts have recognised Orang Asli customary land rights in cases including Adong bin Kuwau and Sagong bin Tasi. The 2019 Kelantan matter was a federal suit filed in the Kota Bharu High Court and related strike-out proceedings, not a Federal Court ruling recognising the rights.
claims.stale_check: No later Federal Court Kelantan ruling matching this description was found.

claims.claim: Perak has not implemented the ruling. No state-level demarcation has occurred.
claims.source: Perak government official page, 11 September 2019, stated the state was in the process of reserving Orang Asli land; SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 criticised Perak's failure to recognise customary land rights. Perak source: https://www.perak.gov.my/index.php/berita-utama/1526-kerajaan-negeri-dalam-proses-rizabkan-tanah-orang-asli-11-september-2019 ; SUHAKAM: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Say Perak had not accepted customary land in the way SUHAKAM and rights advocates urged, while the state said it was reserving/gazetting Orang Asli settlement or roaming areas. Do not say no state-level demarcation occurred without checking current land-office/JAKOA records.
claims.stale_check: Potentially stale: the Perak government stated in September 2019 that reservation work was underway, and the current status may have changed by 2026.

claims.claim: Royal Belum contains 3,000 species including 14 globally threatened mammals.
claims.source: UNESCO Tentative List for Royal Belum State Park says the park contains 14 of the world's most threatened mammals, but the 3,000 figure refers to flowering plants/flora in official tourism sources, not total species. UNESCO: https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/6176 ; Tourism Malaysia: https://www.malaysia.travel/explore/royal-belum-state-park
claims.correction: Correct to: Royal Belum is reported to contain about 3,000 species of flowering plants/flora and 14 of the world's most threatened mammals.
claims.stale_check: No contrary newer official figure found.

claims.claim: Buffer zone logging has fragmented the northern corridor linking Belum to Thailand's Bang Lang National Park.
claims.source: UNESCO confirms Royal Belum borders Thailand and the area has potential transfrontier value; KBA factsheet confirms Hala-Bala/Bang Lang is contiguous with Belum Forest Reserve. Neither primary source verified that buffer-zone logging has fragmented the northern corridor. UNESCO: https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/6176 ; Key Biodiversity Areas factsheet: https://www.keybiodiversityareas.org/site/factsheet/15192
claims.correction: State only that Royal Belum/Belum Forest Reserve is contiguous with Thai protected areas unless a forest-cover, EIA, satellite, or official corridor assessment verifies fragmentation caused by logging.

claims.claim: 8 Temiar villagers face criminal charges for 'obstructing a public road.'
claims.source: SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019 records three Orang Asli arrested/remanded in Kg. Sungai Papan on 23 July 2019; The Malaysian Insight reported three villagers remanded for suspected wrongful restraint/obstruction of vehicles, not eight charged for obstructing a public road. SUHAKAM: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf ; The Malaysian Insight: https://www.themalaysianinsight.com/s/172576
claims.correction: Change the number and legal posture: available sources support three villagers remanded/investigated in July 2019, not eight villagers facing charges for obstructing a public road.
claims.stale_check: No later source found supporting eight charges.

claims.claim: The 'public road' was built by the logging company through community farmland without permission.
claims.source: No primary source found verifying that the road was a public road, that it crossed community farmland, or that it was built without permission. Malaysiakini reported the Perak MB said the barricade was on a logging road and the company opened an alternative path: https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409
claims.correction: Remove or qualify as an allegation unless land-office maps, forestry approvals, court filings, or police reports verify ownership, road status, and lack of permission.

claims.claim: When the road is illegal but the blockade is criminal, the law protects the wrong party.
claims.source: The factual premise that the road was illegal is unverified. Perak MB's statement described the blockade removal as lawful and the logging activity as approved; SUHAKAM criticised authorities' lack of understanding of customary land rights. Malaysiakini: https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409 ; SUHAKAM: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Frame as commentary only after clearly separating verified facts from allegations. Do not assert illegality of the road without a court or regulator finding.
claims.stale_check: No court or regulator finding found.

claims.claim: Orang Asli land rights exist in Federal Court precedent, not in administrative practice.
claims.source: SUHAKAM cites landmark cases Adong bin Kuwau and Sagong bin Tasi, but Sagong was High Court/Court of Appeal, not Federal Court. Perak government said in 2019 it was reserving Orang Asli land, showing some administrative practice exists, though disputed as inadequate. SUHAKAM: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf ; Perak: https://www.perak.gov.my/index.php/berita-utama/1526-kerajaan-negeri-dalam-proses-rizabkan-tanah-orang-asli-11-september-2019
claims.correction: Correct to: Orang Asli customary land rights have been recognised in Malaysian court precedent, but implementation by state administrations remains contested and incomplete.
claims.stale_check: May be stale because administrative implementation status can change; current Perak/JAKOA records should be checked before publication.

claims.claim: Until states demarcate customary land, communities hold rights they cannot exercise.
claims.source: SUHAKAM 2019 says most Peninsular Orang Asli complaints relate to non-recognition of customary land rights and urges governments to recognise native customary land rights, but the absolute statement that communities cannot exercise rights until demarcation is a legal overclaim. https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf
claims.correction: Soften to: Lack of demarcation and state recognition makes Orang Asli customary land rights difficult to enforce in practice.
claims.stale_check: Current demarcation status may have changed since 2019.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: In late 2025, 200 Temiar villagers established a blockade on logging roads entering Royal Belum State Park's buffer zone, halting operations for 87 days before police dismantled it.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Specific date, count, location, duration, and police action could not be verified against a primary source; public evidence found points to 2019 Gerik/Perak events instead.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The Federal Court's 2019 Kelantan ruling recognised Orang Asli customary land rights.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The court level, date, and jurisdiction are wrong. The recognised precedents include Adong bin Kuwau and Sagong bin Tasi; the 2019 Kelantan matter was not a Federal Court ruling recognising customary land rights.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Royal Belum contains 3,000 species including 14 globally threatened mammals.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The 3,000 figure refers to flowering plants/flora, not all species. Pairing it with mammals makes the biodiversity figure imprecise.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: UNESCO Tentative List, Royal Belum State Park, https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/6176 ; Tourism Malaysia, https://www.malaysia.travel/explore/royal-belum-state-park

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 8 Temiar villagers face criminal charges for 'obstructing a public road.'
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Available verified records support three Orang Asli remanded/investigated in 2019, not eight charged, and not the quoted offence description.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The 'public road' was built by the logging company through community farmland without permission.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary evidence was found for the road's legal status, farmland ownership, builder, or lack of permission.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Perak MB statement reported by Malaysiakini, 16 May 2019, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: When the road is illegal but the blockade is criminal, the law protects the wrong party.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The sentence turns an unverified illegality claim into a legal conclusion, while omitting the state government's contrary position that logging and removal were lawful.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Perak MB statement reported by Malaysiakini, 16 May 2019, https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409 ; SUHAKAM Annual Report 2019, https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf

omitted_facts: SUHAKAM recorded the relevant Perak blockades as Kg. Sg Papan and Kg. Cunex in Gerik in 2019, following complaints of logging-company encroachment, river pollution, resource loss, and damage to ancestral graveyards: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf

SUHAKAM says three Orang Asli were arrested/remanded after a 23 July 2019 operation led by the Perak Forestry Department to remove the Kg. Sungai Papan blockade: https://suhakam.org.my/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/SUHAKAM-AR2019-1.pdf

The Perak Menteri Besar's 16 May 2019 statement said the Air Cepam logging activity had permission, notices were issued to remove the barricade, and the state considered the blockade removal lawful: https://www.malaysiakini.com/news/476409

The Perak government stated on 11 September 2019 that it was in the process of reserving Orang Asli land, which complicates the claim that no administrative demarcation or state action existed: https://www.perak.gov.my/index.php/berita-utama/1526-kerajaan-negeri-dalam-proses-rizabkan-tanah-orang-asli-11-september-2019

UNESCO's Royal Belum page describes the park's transboundary conservation context and 14 threatened mammals, but does not verify the analysis's logging-fragmentation claim: https://whc.unesco.org/en/tentativelists/6176
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only 'T4A related issue 1971', which is circular and not a primary source. It does not cite government gazettes, court texts, SUHAKAM, forestry approvals, police records, land-office documents, JAKOA records, UNESCO, or community statements. The institutional range is therefore extremely narrow: no state government position, no human-rights commission record, no court primary material, no forestry regulator source, no community primary testimony, and no conservation primary source. Political spectrum coverage is absent.
overall_assessment: The analysis is not reliable for publication as written. Several core factual elements appear conflated with the documented 2019 Perak/Gerik Temiar blockade rather than a late-2025 Royal Belum event, and the most legally important claim about a 2019 Federal Court Kelantan ruling is incorrect. Some environmental background is partly supportable, but even there the wording overstates or blurs source categories. The piece needs a source rebuild from primary documents before synthesis.
